# ChatGPT Verbatim Copy Lock and Self-Review Protocol

# Boho Digital Services commercial copy lock

Authority: owner directive dated 2026-07-23. This packet applies to all commercial website work orders, including WO-2026-07-23-BOHO-COMMERCIAL-CONTRACT-FOUNDATION-017, WO-2026-07-23-BOHO-HOMEPAGE-VISUAL-SLICE-018, WO-2026-07-23-BOHO-SERVICE-VISUAL-PROPAGATION-019, and WO-2026-07-23-BOHO-COMMERCIAL-SURFACES-QA-020.

## Absolute editorial rule

All public-facing language must be written verbatim by ChatGPT. Codex is an implementation worker, not a copywriter. It may place, encode, escape, split, join, or mechanically transform supplied strings only when the rendered visible text remains byte-for-byte semantically and typographically identical, apart from required HTML escaping, normalized whitespace, typographic apostrophes already supplied by ChatGPT, or insertion of governed glossary-link syntax around the exact supplied visible words.

Codex must not draft, paraphrase, shorten, expand, summarize, clarify, polish, normalize tone, invent a label, invent helper text, invent a caption, invent alt text, invent metadata, invent schema descriptions, invent form success/error copy, invent button text, invent analytics labels visible to users, or rewrite copy to fit a component. If supplied language does not fit the component, change the component or stop and report the conflict. Do not solve a layout problem by editing the sentence.

## Copy-source precedence

1. This packet and later ChatGPT-authored copy addenda.
2. WO-2026-07-23-BOHO-COMMERCIAL-COPY-DECK-V1-009.
3. WO-2026-07-23-BOHO-EVIDENCE-ASSET-REGISTRY-V1-010 for evidence labels, provenance classes, limitations, and captions.
4. WO-2026-07-23-BOHO-COMMERCIAL-VISUAL-ASSET-SPECS-V1-015 for figure titles, labels, explanatory text, captions, and accessible descriptions.
5. Existing governed source only where the higher-precedence packets explicitly preserve it.
6. Generated files are outputs and never editorial authorities.

When two supplied packets conflict, stop and identify the exact strings and locations. Do not choose one silently.

## Public language covered by the lock

The lock includes page titles, meta descriptions, headings, eyebrows, paragraphs, lists, pricing labels, price qualifiers, timing language, CTA labels, navigation labels, footer language, evidence labels, project classifications, captions, figure labels, accessible descriptions, form labels, placeholders, help text, consent text, validation messages, success messages, error messages, empty states, badges, tooltips, disclosure text, schema descriptions, Open Graph text, social descriptions, and any text embedded in SVG or canvas graphics.

Code identifiers, CSS class names, test fixture identifiers, analytics event keys, and internal comments may be technical, but they must not leak into rendered public copy.

## First-pass editorial changes

The commercial implementation must preserve the approved positioning and avoid widening claims. Use these exact governing statements wherever the associated concept is expressed:

- Core position: “Elegant websites and technical SEO, without the agency fog.”
- Operating promise: “Public pricing. Documented work. Clear scope. No mystery retainers.”
- Homepage commercial thesis: “Boho builds and repairs the systems that help people find a business, understand it, trust it, and take the next step.”
- Homepage supporting copy: “That may mean a clearer website, stronger local visibility, a careful migration away from a bad provider, better measurement, or a focused tool that removes repeated manual work. The recommendation starts with the problem, not the service menu.”
- Primary homepage CTA: “Send the Situation”
- Secondary homepage CTA: “See Services and Starting Prices”
- Services-page headline: “Start with the problem. Buy only the work it actually needs.”
- Services-page introduction: “Websites, search, analytics, migration, research, and automation are tools. Boho chooses the smallest useful combination after understanding the business, the customer, the market, and the failure in front of us.”
- Pricing-page headline: “Starting prices without the agency guessing game.”
- Pricing qualification: “These are public planning prices, not automatic quotes. Final scope depends on the current system, the condition of the assets, content readiness, third-party dependencies, urgency, and the amount of uncertainty that must be removed before implementation.”
- Contact-page headline: “Send the situation as it exists.”
- Contact-page introduction: “You do not need to diagnose the website, search market, provider setup, or measurement system before contacting Boho. Describe what is happening, what matters most, and what access or deadlines are already involved. The first useful decision is often choosing what not to buy.”
- Evidence-page headline: “See the work, the method, and the limits of the evidence.”
- Evidence qualification: “Boho does not publish invented client results. Evidence is labeled by source: owned property, public repository or tool, sample document, synthetic demonstration, fictional concept interface, or client work published with permission.”

The five service names must remain exactly:

1. “Local Visibility & Lead Systems”
2. “Websites & Managed Hosting”
3. “Provider Rescue & Migration”
4. “Custom Tools & Automation”
5. “Research, Analytics & Improvement”

The approved starting prices must remain exactly:

- “Initial public review — Free”
- “SEO reporting — Starting at $95 per month”
- “SEO implementation — Starting at $450 per month”
- “Focused website improvement — Starting at $750”
- “New website — Starting at $1,500”
- “Substantial redesign — Starting at $1,500”
- “Provider Rescue Assessment — Starting at $350”
- “Migration assistance — Starting at $1,000”
- “Focused audit or strategy — Starting at $350”
- “Custom discovery — Starting at $500”
- “Focused custom build — Starting at $2,500”

Use this exact credit statement where assessment credits are explained:

“An eligible paid assessment is credited in full toward a larger engagement in the same service category when the later work is approved within the period stated in the proposal. The proposal controls eligibility, timing, and exclusions.”

Use this exact hosting statement wherever hosting inclusion is described:

“Standard managed hosting may be included at no separate hosting charge for an eligible website while the qualifying service relationship remains active and in good standing. Eligibility, platform limits, third-party costs, support boundaries, ownership, and exit handling are defined in the project agreement. This is not a promise of unlimited infrastructure or unlimited support.”

Use this exact results limitation near pricing and ongoing SEO claims:

“Boho does not guarantee rankings, traffic, leads, sales, platform approval, or a specific business result. The work is scoped around documented improvements, measurable signals, and decisions supported by the evidence available.”

## Second-pass self-audit protocol

ChatGPT is the final editorial reviewer. Codex must provide a machine-readable public-copy inventory containing every rendered string changed or added, with source file, route, field, and exact governing packet reference. The inventory must include metadata, schema text, SVG text, alt text, forms, errors, success messages, and mobile-only text.

ChatGPT review occurs in two distinct passes:

### Pass 1 — editorial and commercial review

Verify every changed public string against the governing packets. Reject any unsourced wording, paraphrase, silent shortening, duplicated claim, inconsistent service name, inconsistent price, softened limitation, broadened capability, fabricated proof, unclear concept label, hidden qualifier, or layout-driven copy mutation.

### Pass 2 — adversarial self-audit

Review the accepted Pass 1 candidate again from a skeptical buyer, competitor, regulator, accessibility user, and future maintainer perspective. Specifically challenge:

- whether any claim sounds broader than the evidence;
- whether free, included, starting-at, credited, or managed-hosting language could mislead;
- whether timelines can be mistaken for guarantees;
- whether owned properties or concepts resemble client case studies;
- whether emergency, migration, account recovery, security, SEO, or analytics wording implies authority or access Boho may not possess;
- whether mobile truncation, accordions, aria labels, tooltips, or diagrams alter or conceal material language;
- whether metadata promises more than the visible page;
- whether multiple source files cause inconsistent wording;
- whether a generated file has become an accidental editorial source.

No stage may be accepted until both passes are recorded. A passing build is not editorial acceptance.

## Required implementation evidence

Each implementation report must include:

1. Public-copy inventory.
2. Exact diff of public strings before and after.
3. List of governing packet references used.
4. Statement that Codex authored zero public-facing marketing copy.
5. Any unavoidable mechanical transformations and why visible text is unchanged.
6. Screenshots or rendered-text captures at desktop and mobile widths.
7. Metadata and schema string comparison.
8. Forms-state comparison, including empty, invalid, submitting, success, failure, and rate-limit states when applicable.
9. Explicit unresolved-copy-conflict list, even when empty.

## Stop conditions

Stop without implementing the affected surface when:

- a required public string is absent from ChatGPT-authored material;
- two ChatGPT-authored packets conflict;
- an existing legal, pricing, hosting, privacy, accessibility, or service statement would need substantive rewriting;
- a design requires shortening or paraphrasing supplied copy;
- alt text or accessible description requires editorial judgment not supplied in a packet;
- the implementation would expose placeholder, lorem ipsum, generic AI copy, or worker-authored language;
- a generated artifact changes visible copy unexpectedly.

The worker must report the missing string and exact placement. ChatGPT will supply the wording in a later addendum.

# ChatGPT Editorial Self-Audit V2 and Exact Copy Corrections

# Boho Digital Services commercial editorial self-audit V2

Reviewer: ChatGPT
Date: 2026-07-23
Applies after WO-2026-07-23-BOHO-CHATGPT-VERBATIM-COPY-LOCK-021.

## Audit conclusion

The commercial direction is sound, but the current source and first copy deck contain several classes of wording that should not ship unchanged:

1. Internal governance language appears in public copy. Buyers do not need to see “governed service lanes.”
2. CTA labels drift between problem-oriented, technical, and service-oriented wording. This weakens the conversion path and creates duplicate language across components.
3. Managed-hosting language can be read as a standing entitlement rather than an agreement-bounded provision.
4. Provider-rescue copy occasionally attacks a provider category instead of describing the client’s verifiable situation. Criticism should remain specific and supportable.
5. Some service links use sales verbs such as “Build” or “Explore” when the destination is informational. Link labels should accurately describe the action.
6. The first draft uses “free,” “included,” “credited,” and “starting at” correctly in principle, but those terms must always remain adjacent to their material qualifications.
7. Evidence language needs the source class and limitation visible in the same card or figure, not hidden in a distant disclaimer.
8. Generated TypeScript and reusable component defaults create a risk of copy duplication. Public wording must originate in one governed record and be referenced, not independently repeated.

## Exact corrections

The following replacements override earlier packets and existing source wherever the original string appears in a commercial context.

### Site-wide primary and secondary actions

Replace:
“Talk to Someone Technical”
with:
“Send the Situation”

Replace:
“See What We Build”
with:
“See Services and Starting Prices”

The primary action links to the ordinary contact/start route. The secondary action links to the services or pricing overview selected by the approved information architecture. Codex may select the correct approved URL but may not alter either label.

### Services overview

Replace eyebrow:
“Five governed service lanes”
with:
“Five ways Boho can help”

Replace heading:
“Choose the lane after naming the problem.”
with:
“Name the problem before choosing the service.”

Replace the page headline with:
“Start with the problem. Buy only the work it actually needs.”

Replace the page introduction with:
“Websites, search, analytics, migration, research, and automation are tools. Boho chooses the smallest useful combination after understanding the business, the customer, the market, and the failure in front of us.”

Replace service link labels exactly:

- “Build the Lead System” → “See Local Visibility & Lead Systems”
- “Plan the Website System” → “See Websites & Managed Hosting”
- “Plan the Rescue” → “See Provider Rescue & Migration”
- “Explore Practical Automation” → “See Custom Tools & Automation”
- “Start With Evidence” → “See Research, Analytics & Improvement”

Preserve this exact closing heading:
“Bring the business problem. Boho will help name the service.”

Preserve this exact closing body:
“You do not need to diagnose the website, search market, provider setup, or measurement system before contacting us. Send the situation as it exists. The first useful decision is often choosing what not to buy.”

### Homepage

Use exactly:

Eyebrow:
“Websites, search, and technical systems”

Headline:
“Elegant websites and technical SEO, without the agency fog.”

Body paragraph 1:
“Boho builds and repairs the systems that help people find a business, understand it, trust it, and take the next step.”

Body paragraph 2:
“That may mean a clearer website, stronger local visibility, a careful migration away from a bad provider, better measurement, or a focused tool that removes repeated manual work. The recommendation starts with the problem, not the service menu.”

Primary CTA:
“Send the Situation”

Secondary CTA:
“See Services and Starting Prices”

Trust line:
“Public pricing. Documented work. Clear scope. No mystery retainers.”

Do not append unsupported superlatives, geographic dominance claims, outcome claims, client counts, years of experience, or availability claims.

### Websites & Managed Hosting

Use exactly:

Headline:
“Websites built to explain the business, earn trust, and remain understandable to operate.”

Introduction:
“Boho designs, redesigns, and supports websites for businesses that need clearer services, stronger identity, better search foundations, cleaner lead paths, and less infrastructure fog.”

Preserve:
“A beautiful page that hides the service, buries the phone number, or collapses on mobile is a poster with hosting fees.”

Replace any broader hosting promise with exactly:
“Standard managed hosting may be included at no separate hosting charge for an eligible website while the qualifying service relationship remains active and in good standing. Eligibility, platform limits, third-party costs, support boundaries, ownership, and exit handling are defined in the project agreement. This is not a promise of unlimited infrastructure or unlimited support.”

Use exactly for the focused-project alternative:
“Not every website needs a reinvention. When the current foundation is sound, a focused improvement project may be more useful and less expensive than a full redesign.”

### Provider Rescue & Migration

Preserve headline:
“Leave the bad provider without setting the useful parts on fire.”

Replace any generalized description of “messy SEO companies” or categorical attack with exactly:
“Boho helps businesses leave unsuitable, uncooperative, or poorly documented providers while protecting the accounts, content, URLs, measurement history, and working systems worth keeping.”

Use exactly:
“A provider rescue begins with an ownership and dependency map. Boho does not claim access it does not have, bypass account security, impersonate the owner, or guarantee that a registrar, host, platform, or former provider will approve a recovery request.”

Use exactly for urgency:
“Urgent work is accepted only when the required access, authority, capacity, and risk controls are available. Priority work may cost more, and urgency does not remove the need for verification or a rollback plan.”

### Local Visibility & Lead Systems

Use exactly:

Headline:
“Make the business easier to find, easier to understand, and easier to contact.”

Introduction:
“Boho connects local discovery, service pages, business-profile accuracy, trust signals, inquiry paths, and measurement around the qualified actions that matter to the business.”

Use exactly for limitations:
“Boho does not guarantee rankings, map placement, traffic, calls, leads, sales, reviews, platform approval, or a specific business result. The work is evaluated through documented changes, measurable signals, and decisions supported by the evidence available.”

### Research, Analytics & Improvement

Use exactly:

Headline:
“Find out what is wrong before paying to fix the wrong thing.”

Introduction:
“Boho reviews the system, the evidence, and the market to separate visible symptoms from the underlying problem. The result is a prioritized record of what to fix, what to measure, what to leave alone, and what remains uncertain.”

Use exactly:
“An audit is an evidence-based review, not a certification, legal opinion, security guarantee, appraisal, or promise that every hidden defect will be found.”

### Custom Tools & Automation

Use exactly:

Headline:
“Replace repeated manual work with a focused system people can understand.”

Introduction:
“Boho designs small tools, integrations, and automations for work that is repetitive, fragile, difficult to audit, or unnecessarily dependent on one person remembering every step.”

Use exactly:
“Custom work begins with discovery because automation can make a sound process faster or a bad process fail at scale. Boho will not automate an unclear, unauthorized, unsafe, or economically pointless workflow merely because it can be coded.”

### Pricing

Use exactly:

Headline:
“Starting prices without the agency guessing game.”

Qualification:
“These are public planning prices, not automatic quotes. Final scope depends on the current system, the condition of the assets, content readiness, third-party dependencies, urgency, and the amount of uncertainty that must be removed before implementation.”

Credit statement:
“An eligible paid assessment is credited in full toward a larger engagement in the same service category when the later work is approved within the period stated in the proposal. The proposal controls eligibility, timing, and exclusions.”

Results limitation:
“Boho does not guarantee rankings, traffic, leads, sales, platform approval, or a specific business result. The work is scoped around documented improvements, measurable signals, and decisions supported by the evidence available.”

Every price must display “Starting at” unless the item is explicitly “Free.” Do not replace “Starting at” with “from,” “as low as,” “only,” or any urgency-oriented phrasing.

### Evidence and Work

Use exactly:

Headline:
“See the work, the method, and the limits of the evidence.”

Introduction:
“Boho does not publish invented client results. Evidence is labeled by source: owned property, public repository or tool, sample document, synthetic demonstration, fictional concept interface, or client work published with permission.”

Every evidence card must include these visible field labels:

- “Source class”
- “What this demonstrates”
- “What this does not demonstrate”
- “Current status”
- “Open the evidence”

Do not use “case study” for owned properties, concepts, samples, or synthetic demonstrations.

### Contact and Start

Use exactly:

Headline:
“Send the situation as it exists.”

Introduction:
“You do not need to diagnose the website, search market, provider setup, or measurement system before contacting Boho. Describe what is happening, what matters most, and what access or deadlines are already involved. The first useful decision is often choosing what not to buy.”

Primary submit label:
“Send the Situation”

Success heading:
“Your message was sent.”

Success body:
“Boho will review the situation and reply with the next useful question or step. A submitted form is not an accepted project, emergency response, quote, or guarantee of availability.”

Generic failure heading:
“The message was not sent.”

Generic failure body:
“Your entries are still on this page. Review any field-specific message and try again. For urgent provider or website incidents, use the emergency route only when the incident meets the conditions stated there.”

Privacy helper text:
“Do not send passwords, private keys, recovery codes, payment-card information, medical information, or other secrets through this form.”

### Metadata and schema

Metadata must describe the visible page without adding a result claim, geographic claim, certification, availability promise, or capability absent from the visible content. Schema descriptions must reuse approved visible copy or a ChatGPT-authored exact excerpt. Codex must not generate marketing descriptions from page content.

## Second-pass adversarial findings

The following are mandatory review findings, not optional polish:

1. Reject “governed,” “lane,” “ecosystem,” “full-service,” “end-to-end,” “world-class,” “best-in-class,” “turnkey,” “seamless,” and similar agency abstractions in public commercial copy unless ChatGPT explicitly supplies the exact sentence.
2. Reject CTA labels that imply an action the destination cannot perform.
3. Reject hosting language that separates “included” from its eligibility and agreement qualifiers.
4. Reject pricing cards that visually de-emphasize “Starting at,” billing cadence, or major exclusions.
5. Reject evidence cards whose source class or limitation is hidden behind hover, accordion, tooltip, or secondary navigation.
6. Reject a concept interface that could reasonably be mistaken for client work at first glance.
7. Reject metadata that makes a broader promise than the visible page.
8. Reject any worker-authored alt text. ChatGPT must supply exact alt text or the graphic must be implemented as decorative only when that is semantically correct.
9. Reject silent changes caused by Markdown normalization, smart-quote conversion, generator templates, component defaults, responsive truncation, or CSS line clamping.
10. Reject any new public string not present in a ChatGPT-authored packet.

## Acceptance rule

A stage is editorially acceptable only when the rendered-copy inventory proves that every new or changed public string maps to a ChatGPT-authored packet and the second-pass adversarial review records no unresolved finding. Codex passing tests, lint, build, accessibility tooling, or screenshot comparison is necessary but not sufficient.

# ChatGPT Verbatim Service Timeline Copy

# Boho Digital Services verbatim timeline copy

Author: ChatGPT
Status: Binding public-copy addendum for commercial work orders 023–026

Codex may not paraphrase, compress, or convert these ranges into guarantees. Material qualifications must appear in the same visible section as the range.

## Site-wide timeline qualification

Use exactly:

“Timelines are planning ranges, not guarantees. Work begins after the required access, content, approvals, and initial payment are available. Provider delays, platform reviews, missing assets, scope changes, and client response time can extend the calendar.”

Use exactly near outcome timing:

“The work schedule describes when Boho can complete agreed tasks. Search engines, platforms, providers, markets, and customers may take longer to process or respond to those changes.”

## Inquiry and initial review

Use exactly:

“Boho normally replies to a standard inquiry within two business days. A reply may ask for clarification, recommend a smaller first step, or explain that the project is not a fit.”

Use exactly for the free public review:

“A straightforward free public-information review is normally completed within two business days after the required website and business context are received. It is orientation, not a complete audit, private-account review, recovery engagement, implementation project, quote, or guarantee of availability.”

## Local Visibility & Lead Systems

Use exactly:

“Initial baseline and priority plan: usually 5–10 business days after required access and business information are available.”

“First implementation cycle: usually begins within 2–4 weeks after scope approval, depending on access, technical condition, content readiness, and the amount of work included.”

“Ongoing work: organized in monthly cycles with a named priority, completed-work record, verification, limitations, and next decision.”

“Search evaluation: meaningful movement often requires several months of observation. No specific ranking, traffic, lead, or revenue timeline is guaranteed.”

## Websites & Managed Hosting

Use exactly:

“Initial recommendation and scope: usually 2–5 business days after the current site, business requirements, and available assets are reviewed.”

“Small or straightforward website: usually 4–8 weeks after required access, content decisions, assets, approvals, and initial payment are available.”

“Substantial redesign or migration: usually 6–12 weeks. Large content inventories, custom integrations, provider dependencies, or delayed approvals may require more time.”

“Post-launch verification: normally 7–14 days for the checks stated in the project scope. This observation period is not a promise that every third-party system will process the launch within that time.”

## Provider Rescue & Migration

Use exactly:

“Initial risk review: usually 1–3 business days after the known accounts, provider, platform, and failure are identified.”

“Ownership and dependency inventory: usually 2–5 business days when the required information and authorized access are available.”

“Straightforward migration: often 1–3 weeks after access and scope are confirmed.”

“Complex provider rescue or migration: often 2–6 weeks or longer. Former-provider cooperation, registrar or platform review, account recovery, DNS dependencies, content condition, and legal ownership disputes can control the schedule.”

“Boho cannot guarantee access recovery, provider cooperation, platform approval, or a specific restoration time.”

## Research, Analytics & Improvement

Use exactly:

“Focused review: usually 5–10 business days after the agreed evidence and access are available.”

“Large technical, analytics, market, or multi-property audit: usually 2–4 weeks.”

“Findings discussion: normally offered within two business days after delivery.”

“The timeline covers the agreed review and report. It does not guarantee that every hidden defect, historical cause, or unavailable data source will be discovered.”

## Custom Tools & Automation

Use exactly:

“Initial feasibility review: usually 2–5 business days after the workflow, users, inputs, outputs, and constraints are described.”

“Paid discovery, when required: usually 1–2 weeks.”

“Small focused automation or internal tool: often 2–6 weeks after discovery and approval.”

“Larger custom systems are scheduled only after discovery. Integration access, external APIs, security requirements, testing, data migration, and human-review boundaries can materially change the timeline.”

## Emergency work

Use exactly:

“Emergency requests are triaged as capacity, authority, access, and risk controls allow. Submitting a request does not guarantee immediate response, acceptance, restoration, or resolution.”

“Priority work may cost more. Urgency does not remove the need for authorized access, verification, documentation, or a rollback plan.”

## Presentation requirements

- Show the applicable range near the starting price and scope summary.
- Do not hide the qualification in a tooltip, accordion, footer, or separate legal page.
- Do not display a single completion date unless a written project agreement supplies one.
- Do not use countdowns, artificial urgency, capacity claims, or availability claims.
- Do not replace “usually,” “normally,” “often,” “planning ranges,” or “may” with stronger language.
- Do not combine implementation timing with external outcome timing.
- Metadata may mention that timelines are published, but must not state a guaranteed duration.
